subsystem: Linux kernel rtlwifi (Realtek 802.11 wireless) driver — the P2P / Wi-Fi-Direct power-save parser rtl_p2p_noa_ie(). It walks the variable information-element (IE) area of a received 802.11 management frame, locates the Wi-Fi-Alliance P2P vendor-specific IE (element ID 221, OUI 50:6f:9a:09), then within it parses the Notice-of-Absence (NoA, attribute ID 12) attribute and copies its schedule descriptors into the per-device rtl_p2p_ps_info structure used to drive power-save state.
provenance: Identified from rtlwifi-specific names: struct ieee80211_hw / ieee80211_mgmt (mac80211), struct rtl_priv, rtl_priv(hw), struct rtl_p2p_ps_info and rtlpriv->psc.p2p_ps_info, the READEF1BYTE/READEF2BYTE/READEF4BYTE little-endian accessor macros, RT_TRACE with COMP_INIT/COMP_FW/DBG_LOUD, the P2P_PS_NONE/P2P_PS_CTWINDOW/P2P_PS_NOA/P2P_PS_ENABLE/P2P_PS_DISABLE constants, P2P_MAX_NOA_NUM, and rtl_p2p_ps_cmd(). This is drivers/net/wireless/realtek/rtlwifi/ps.c::rtl_p2p_noa_ie. The sample banner ('primevul_c_train_2699_fixed', file train_0164_cwe120.c) further marks it as a fixed variant of a known buffer-overflow sample.
trust_boundary: The two parameters data and len describe the raw bytes of a received 802.11 management frame (beacon / probe-response carrying P2P IEs). This is over-the-air, fully attacker-controllable input: any radio in range can transmit a frame with arbitrary IE bytes, element IDs, lengths, the vendor OUI, and NoA attribute contents. Everything derived from the buffer — pos[0]/pos[1] (element id and length), the OUI bytes, ie[0] (attribute id), ie[1]/ie[2] and noa_len (attribute length), noa_num, and the per-descriptor bytes — is hostile. The destination rtlpriv->psc.p2p_ps_info holds FIXED-size arrays sized P2P_MAX_NOA_NUM, so attacker-chosen counts/lengths crossing that bound is the core hazard.

what_the_code_does.explanation: The entry point takes the mac80211 hardware handle hw, an opaque pointer data to the start of the received management frame, and its byte length len. data and len are the untrusted-input boundary: they carry the full over-the-air frame. Because data is typed void*, all structure and bounds interpretation is done manually below, so the safety of every access rests on the arithmetic in this function rather than on the type system.

rtlpriv is the driver-private context; mgmt reinterprets the untrusted buffer as an 802.11 management frame so the fixed header can be skipped; p2pinfo aliases the persistent per-device NoA state that this function writes into (the fixed-size destination arrays live here). pos/end/ie are the running cursors for the IE walk. noa_len is a 16-bit attribute length read from the wire. p2p_oui_ie_type is the constant 4-byte P2P OUI+type (50 6f 9a 09) used to recognise the vendor IE. noa_num, index, i, noa_index are all u8 counters/offsets — note index is only 8 bits, so it saturates at 255. find_p2p_ie / find_p2p_ps_ie track whether the two IEs were seen, and drive the cleanup at the end.

pos is set to the start of the variable IE region, i.e. just past the fixed beacon header inside the frame. end = data + len is the one-past-the-last valid byte, the sole bound used by every subsequent check. ie is initialised NULL and is later set to the first byte of the P2P attribute area. Note there is no explicit check that len is at least offsetof(...variable) — if len is smaller than the fixed beacon header, pos can start at or beyond end, in which case the loops below simply do nothing (they compare pos/ie against end).

This is the outer IE scan. The loop guard pos + 1 < end ensures pos[0] (element id) and pos[1] (element length) are readable. Line 19 is the crucial per-IE bound: pos + 2 + pos[1] > end rejects the frame if the declared IE body (2-byte header + pos[1] payload) would run past end; pos[1] is a single byte so it maxes at 255. When the element id is 221 (vendor-specific) and the length exceeds 4, the code compares the first 4 payload bytes against the P2P OUI/type; this 4-byte memcmp is safe because pos[1] > 4 combined with the line-19 check guarantees at least pos[2..5] are in-bounds. On a match, ie is set to pos + 6 (past the 2-byte header and the 4-byte OUI) and the scan stops. Otherwise pos advances by the full IE size 2 + pos[1] and the walk continues.

If no P2P vendor IE was located, the function returns without touching NoA state. Otherwise find_p2p_ie is recorded (it gates the final cleanup) and ie now points at the first P2P attribute inside the vendor IE, ready for the inner attribute walk.

The inner loop walks P2P attributes. Each attribute is [1-byte id][2-byte little-endian length][payload]. The guard ie + 1 < end ensures ie[0] and ie[1] are readable. noa_len is read as a full 16-bit little-endian value from ie[1..2], so it can be up to 65535. Line 37 is the inner bound check, but it validates only ie + 3 + ie[1] — using the single low byte ie[1], NOT the 16-bit noa_len that actually governs how many descriptor bytes will be consumed and how far ie is advanced at line 95. This asymmetry is the key contract weakness (see what_could_go_wrong).

Attribute id 12 is the Notice-of-Absence attribute. A NoA attribute body is a 2-byte header (index + CTWindow/OppPS) followed by an array of 13-byte NoA descriptors, so the code requires (noa_len - 2) to be a multiple of 13, else it logs and returns. noa_num is then the number of descriptors, (noa_len - 2) / 13. Because noa_len is a u16, in the '(noa_len - 2)' expression it is promoted to signed int, so for noa_len 0 or 1 the result is -2 or -1 (the %13 test is non-zero and the function returns) rather than a huge unsigned wrap. Lines 49-50 are the security-critical guard added in this fixed variant: noa_num is clamped to P2P_MAX_NOA_NUM so the write loop below cannot exceed the fixed destination arrays. Without this clamp this is the classic rtlwifi NoA overflow.

noa_index is read from ie[3] (the NoA 'index' byte) and used to detect whether the schedule changed; only a new index (or a device currently in P2P_PS_NONE) triggers an update. ie[4] holds OppPS (top bit) and CTWindow (low 7 bits), which are stored. The clamped noa_num is committed to p2pinfo->noa_num. Reads of ie[3] and ie[4] rely on those offsets being inside the buffer — they are covered only if the line-37 bound actually validated at least 5 bytes of body, which is guaranteed for well-formed lengths but see the length-mismatch risk.

The descriptor loop copies noa_num records into the four parallel per-device arrays noa_count_type[], noa_duration[], noa_interval[], noa_start_time[] (each dimensioned P2P_MAX_NOA_NUM). index starts at 5 (past the 2-byte NoA header) and advances 1+4+4+4 = 13 bytes per descriptor. The 1-byte read uses ie+index (correct byte offset), but the three 4-byte reads use (__le32 *)ie+index: because the cast binds tighter than +, index is scaled by sizeof(__le32)=4, so these read at byte offset 4*index rather than index — an offset-computation defect that both mis-reads the schedule and pushes the read cursor far past the validated region. The write side i is bounded by the clamped noa_num, so the destination-array writes are in range in this fixed variant.

Based on the parsed OppPS bit and descriptor count, the driver sets p2p_ps_mode and issues firmware power-save commands via rtl_p2p_ps_cmd: CTWindow mode enables PS only if firmware is already in power-save, a positive NoA count enables NOA power-save, and otherwise a previously-active mode is disabled. The break exits the attribute walk once the NoA attribute has been handled. These are the observable side effects an attacker's crafted NoA can drive.

For non-NoA attributes the cursor advances by 3 + noa_len (the full 16-bit length) to the next attribute; note this uses noa_len while the loop's bound check at line 37 used only ie[1], so the advance and the validation are computed from different quantities. Finally, if a P2P IE was present but no NoA sub-IE was found, and a power-save mode is active, the driver disables P2P power-save to fail safe.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: The inner bound check validates only ie + 3 + ie[1], where ie[1] is the LOW byte of the 16-bit length, but noa_len (read as the full little-endian 16-bit value from ie[1..2]) is what actually drives noa_num, the descriptor read loop, and the ie advance at line 95. An attacker sets the high length byte ie[2] non-zero (e.g. ie[1]=0x00, ie[2]=0x01 so noa_len=256) so that line 37 only guarantees ~3 bytes are in-bounds while noa_len claims 256. noa_num then becomes (256-2)/13 = 19, clamped to P2P_MAX_NOA_NUM, and the loop reads ie[3], ie[4] and multiple descriptor bytes from memory the check never validated against end — an out-of-bounds read of adjacent kernel memory (over the air, remotely triggerable). Invariant: the SAME length used to size the payload (noa_len) must be the one bounded against end before any body byte is read; the check must use noa_len, not ie[1]. This mismatch is the sharpest residual defect even in this 'fixed' variant.

Operator-precedence defect: the intended source address is ie + index (byte offset), matching the 1-byte read at line 65 which correctly writes REST as ie+index. But (__le32 *)ie+index parses as ((__le32 *)ie) + index, so pointer arithmetic scales index by sizeof(__le32)=4. With index reaching 5,6,10,14,... the 4-byte reads land at byte offsets 24,40,56,... rather than 6,10,14, walking tens of bytes past the NoA payload and past the line-37 validated span. Impact: out-of-bounds read of kernel memory into the stored schedule (info leak into driver state / power-save timing) and wrong parsing. Invariant: the read cursor must be a byte pointer; use READEF4BYTE((__le32 *)(ie+index)). This is a latent bug independent of the length-mismatch above and compounds the over-read.

This is the central overflow contract. noa_num is attacker-derived from a 16-bit wire length and indexes four FIXED-size destination arrays (p2pinfo->noa_count_type/noa_duration/noa_interval/noa_start_time, each sized P2P_MAX_NOA_NUM). If the clamp at lines 49-50 were absent or wrong, the loop at 63-76 writes noa_num descriptors (up to ~5000 for noa_len=65535) straight past those arrays inside rtl_priv — a remote out-of-bounds WRITE (CWE-787), which is exactly CVE-2019-17666. In this fixed sample the clamp is present, so the writes are bounded PROVIDED P2P_MAX_NOA_NUM equals the declared array dimension. The residual thing to verify is that the array declarations in the header truly use P2P_MAX_NOA_NUM (an off-by-one or a differently-sized array would re-open the overflow).

noa_len is u16, so noa_len - 2 is evaluated in int and does NOT wrap to a huge unsigned value: for noa_len 0/1 the result is negative and the %13 test rejects, for noa_len 2 noa_num is 0 and the loop body is skipped. So there is no unsigned-underflow overflow here per se. The remaining subtlety is only that this length validates the descriptor count while the actual buffer bound (line 37) is computed from a different byte — captured by the top finding — so the %13 gate gives false confidence that the body is well-formed and fully present. Invariant: length used for structural validation and length used for bounds validation must be identical.

There is no explicit check that len is at least the fixed offset of the beacon variable field, nor that data/len came from a beacon (the union member u.beacon is assumed). If len is smaller than that header offset, pos > end and the loops harmlessly do nothing; but the code trusts the caller to have already validated a minimum frame length and the correct frame subtype. Invariant to confirm in the caller: len covers the full 802.11 + fixed beacon header before this function runs, and this path is only reached for frames whose union member is actually beacon.variable, so mgmt->u.beacon.variable is the right offset.

summary: Remote, over-the-air 802.11 IE parser for the rtlwifi P2P Notice-of-Absence attribute. The headline heap/array overflow of CVE-2019-17666 is mitigated here by the noa_num clamp to P2P_MAX_NOA_NUM (lines 49-50). Two real defects remain: the inner bound check at line 37 validates only the low length byte ie[1] while the full 16-bit noa_len drives consumption (out-of-bounds READ, CWE-125), and the (__le32 *)ie+index operator-precedence bug scales the read offset by 4 causing further over-reads and mis-parsing (CWE-125). Overall medium risk given the write path is now clamped.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: rtl_p2p_noa_ie(struct ieee80211_hw *hw, void *data, unsigned int len) (lines 2-3)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Trust boundary: data/len are attacker-controlled over-the-air frame bytes parsed manually.
Reinterpret cast of the untrusted buffer; safety is enforced by the manual pos/end bounds, not here.
Destination of the descriptor writes; fixed P2P_MAX_NOA_NUM-sized arrays paired with an attacker count.
16-bit attacker length that drives count/read/advance but is not the value used in the line-37 bound check.
Constant OUI compared with a length-guarded 4-byte memcmp; no attacker write, read is bounded by pos[1]>4 plus line 19.
8-bit descriptor count and byte offset; noa_num indexes fixed arrays, index is the read cursor into the wire buffer.
No explicit minimum-length/subtype guard; relies on caller having validated the frame.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: while (pos + 1 < end) ... if (pos + 2 + pos[1] > end) return; (lines 18-20)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Outer IE bound check is correct: guard makes pos[0]/pos[1] readable and rejects IEs that exceed end before use.
pos[1]>4 together with line 19 guarantees pos[2..5] in-bounds, so the 4-byte OUI memcmp is safe.
Advances derived from the outer, correctly-bounded length; ie start is re-bounded by the inner loop guard.
Guard correctly ensures ie[0]/ie[1] readable before the length read; issue is the subsequent bound value, not this guard.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: noa_len = READEF2BYTE((__le16 *)&ie[1]); vs if (ie + 3 + ie[1] > end) (lines 36-37)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Length used to validate (ie[1], low byte only) differs from length used to consume (16-bit noa_len) -> out-of-bounds read.
Attribute-type discriminator; no memory-safety contract by itself.
int promotion prevents unsigned wrap, but this length structurally validates the body while a different length bounds it; documents why no underflow exists here.
The clamp that mitigates CVE-2019-17666; correctness depends on P2P_MAX_NOA_NUM matching the array dimensions.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: noa_index = ie[3]; opp_ps = ie[4] >> 7; ctwindow = ie[4] & 0x7F (lines 53,59,60)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Fixed low-offset reads (bytes 3-4) into the attribute; in-bounds for any well-formed length and covered when the bound check is correct.
Descriptor copy loop: writes bounded by clamped noa_num (fixed), reads governed by index into the wire buffer (see over-read findings).
Correct byte-offset read; contrast case showing the intended addressing that the 4-byte reads get wrong.
Operator precedence scales index by 4 -> reads at byte offset 4*index, out-of-bounds over-read and mis-parse.
Advance uses full noa_len while the loop bounded only ie[1]; part of the length-mismatch surface (next-iteration guard prevents deref but confirms the inconsistency).
The clamp bound; must equal the declared size of the four noa_* arrays or the write overflow re-opens.
Little-endian accessor macros; they read exactly 1/2/4 bytes at the supplied address — hazard is the address arithmetic, not the macros.
Logging; rtlpriv is a valid device handle here, no NULL-deref-at-log-site pattern.
Downstream power-save command; the attacker influence is only which enum is passed, not a memory-safety issue in this file.

findings.title: Inner bound check uses low length byte ie[1] while 16-bit noa_len drives consumption -> out-of-bounds read
findings.invariant: The exact length that determines how many payload bytes are read (the full 16-bit noa_len) must be the length validated against end before any payload byte is dereferenced; validating only the low byte ie[1] is insufficient.
findings.failure_mode: An attacker crafts the NoA attribute so the high length byte ie[2] is non-zero (e.g. ie[1]=0x00, ie[2]=0x01 => noa_len=256). Line 37 then only ensures ~3 bytes past ie are in-bounds, but noa_num=(256-2)/13 and the descriptor loop (plus ie[3]/ie[4]) read further bytes the check never bounded against end, reading adjacent kernel memory. Even with the noa_num clamp the read still walks past the validated span. Remotely triggerable by any frame in radio range.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm whether line 37 should read 'ie + 3 + noa_len > end' (full length) and that ie[3]/ie[4] and every ie+index access is proven <= end using the same length that computes noa_num.

findings.title: Operator-precedence bug: (__le32 *)ie+index scales the read offset by 4 -> over-read and mis-parse
findings.invariant: index is a byte offset into the wire buffer; the 4-byte reads must dereference the byte address ie+index, i.e. READEF4BYTE((__le32 *)(ie+index)), so the cast applies AFTER the addition.
findings.failure_mode: Because the cast binds tighter than '+', (__le32 *)ie+index equals ((__le32 *)ie)+index and scales index by sizeof(__le32)=4. With index=6,10,14 the reads land at byte offsets 24,40,56 instead of 6,10,14, reading tens of bytes past the NoA payload (and past the line-37 validated region) into unrelated memory. This both leaks/uses out-of-bounds bytes as the stored NoA schedule and corrupts the parsed timing values. Compare the correct byte-scaled read on line 65 (ie+index).
findings.what_to_confirm: Verify the intended addressing: is the parenthesization meant to be (__le32 *)(ie+index)? If so, the reads are 4x off; confirm against the P2P NoA descriptor layout (count_type at +0, duration at +1, interval at +5, start_time at +9 within each 13-byte record).

findings.title: Attacker-controlled NoA descriptor count writes into fixed P2P_MAX_NOA_NUM arrays (CVE-2019-17666); mitigated only by the clamp
findings.anchor: noa_num = (noa_len - 2) / 13; if (noa_num > P2P_MAX_NOA_NUM) noa_num = P2P_MAX_NOA_NUM; ... for (i = 0; i < noa_num; i++) p2pinfo->noa_*[i] = ...
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds write / buffer overflow (unchecked count into fixed array)
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0164_cwe120.c (writes); array declarations in struct rtl_p2p_ps_info header (file not provided)
findings.invariant: noa_num must never exceed the declared dimension of noa_count_type/noa_duration/noa_interval/noa_start_time, and that dimension must equal P2P_MAX_NOA_NUM for the clamp to be sufficient.
findings.failure_mode: A large noa_len yields a huge descriptor count (up to ~5000 for noa_len=65535). Without the clamp the loop writes far past the four fixed arrays inside rtl_priv, a remote heap/struct overflow — exactly CVE-2019-17666. In THIS fixed variant lines 49-50 clamp noa_num to P2P_MAX_NOA_NUM, so the writes are bounded provided the arrays are actually sized P2P_MAX_NOA_NUM. Residual risk is an array whose size diverges from P2P_MAX_NOA_NUM (off-by-one or refactor drift) re-opening the write overflow.
findings.what_to_confirm: In the header, confirm noa_count_type[], noa_duration[], noa_interval[], noa_start_time[] are each declared with dimension P2P_MAX_NOA_NUM (not a smaller literal), so the line-50 clamp fully bounds the write loop.

findings.title: Inconsistent attribute advance: ie += 3 + noa_len uses the full length the bound check never validated
findings.invariant: The cursor advance and the per-iteration bound check must use the same attribute length; advancing by noa_len while bounding by ie[1] means the cursor can jump inconsistently relative to what was validated.
findings.failure_mode: For a non-NoA attribute the cursor advances by 3+noa_len (full 16-bit), which can be much larger than the 3+ie[1] span validated at line 37. The next-iteration guard 'while (ie + 1 < end)' prevents a dereference at the overshot position (the loop simply ends), so this is not itself an over-read, but it is the same length-inconsistency root as finding 1 and confirms noa_len/ie[1] are used interchangeably by mistake.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the intended attribute length width (1 vs 2 bytes) and make the guard, the descriptor sizing, and the advance all use that single width consistently.

findings.title: No explicit minimum-length / frame-subtype guard before treating data as a beacon
findings.where_it_lives: caller in the rtlwifi RX path (file not provided)
findings.invariant: len must be at least offsetof(struct ieee80211_mgmt, u.beacon.variable) and the frame must actually be the subtype whose union member is u.beacon before this offset is used.
findings.failure_mode: The function trusts that data/len describe a beacon large enough to contain the fixed header. If len is smaller, pos starts at or beyond end and both loops become no-ops (no memory-safety impact here), but the assumption is implicit; a caller feeding a differently-shaped management frame could point pos at the wrong offset. Impact is limited because all reads are re-bounded against end, so this is defense-in-depth rather than an exploitable bug in this file.
findings.what_to_confirm: Verify the RX caller validates minimum frame length and the management subtype before invoking rtl_p2p_noa_ie, so mgmt->u.beacon.variable is the correct IE offset.

audit_checklist: Change the inner bound check (line 37) to validate the full 16-bit noa_len (ie + 3 + noa_len > end), not ie[1], before reading ie[3], ie[4], or any descriptor byte.
Fix the operator precedence in the 4-byte reads (lines 68/71/74) to READEF4BYTE((__le32 *)(ie+index)) so index is a byte offset, not a 4-scaled element index.
Confirm noa_count_type[], noa_duration[], noa_interval[], noa_start_time[] in struct rtl_p2p_ps_info are each dimensioned exactly P2P_MAX_NOA_NUM so the line-50 clamp fully bounds the write loop.
Make the attribute-length width consistent everywhere: the loop guard, noa_num sizing, and the ie advance (line 95) must all use the same length quantity.

Prove that ie[3] and ie[4] (NoA header bytes) and the maximum index reached in the loop are all <= end using the same validated length; add an explicit remaining-bytes check before the descriptor loop.
Confirm the RX caller validates a minimum frame length and the correct management subtype before calling rtl_p2p_noa_ie, so mgmt->u.beacon.variable is a valid offset.
Check that (noa_len - 2) is evaluated with the intended signedness and that noa_len < 2 is rejected (it currently is, via int promotion and the %13 test).
